Control Arm.evidence: In the 'Methods' section, under 'Randomisation and masking', it states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) to either standard of care (control group) or standard of care plus abiraterone (in the abiraterone trial) or plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial). Each trial randomly assigned patients to its own control group; there were no overlapping controls.' This clearly identifies 'standard of care' as the control group.

Treatment Arm - N.value: 501 (standard of care plus abiraterone); 462 (standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide)
Treatment Arm - N.evidence: In the 'Findings' section, it states: 'Between Nov 15, 2011, and Jan 17, 2014, 1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial.' and 'Between July 29, 2014, and March 31, 2016, 916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' These numbers represent the participants in the experimental arms of the two trials.

Median Follow-Up Duration (mo).value: 96 months (IQR 86–107) in the abiraterone trial and 72 months (61–74) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial
Median Follow-Up Duration (mo).evidence: Found in the 'Findings' section on page 3, which states: 'Median follow-up was 96 months (IQR 86–107) in the abiraterone trial and 72 months (61–74) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.'

Control Arm - N.value: Abiraterone trial: 502; Abiraterone and enzalutamide trial: 454
Control Arm - N.evidence: In the 'Findings' section, it states: 'Between Nov 15, 2011, and Jan 17, 2014, 1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial.' and 'Between July 29, 2014, and March 31, 2016, 916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' These numbers represent the participants in the control arms of the two trials.

Treatment Arm(s).value: Standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg; Standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg
Treatment Arm(s).evidence: The 'Methods' section states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care ... or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).' These are the two experimental treatment arms.

Number of Arms Included.evidence: The 'Methods' section states, 'We analysed two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol, with no overlapping controls'. It then describes the random assignment for each trial: 'standard of care... or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide... (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial)'. This indicates two arms for the abiraterone trial (control and experimental) and two distinct arms for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial (control and experimental), totaling 4 arms.

Total Participants - N.evidence: In the 'Findings' section, it states: 'Between Nov 15, 2011, and Jan 17, 2014, 1003 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=502) or standard of care plus abiraterone (n=501) in the abiraterone trial.' and 'Between July 29, 2014, and March 31, 2016, 916 patients were randomly assigned to standard of care (n=454) or standard of care plus abiraterone and enzalutamide (n=462) in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial.' The total number of participants for these two trials is 1003 + 916 = 1919.

Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.evidence: In the 'Methods' section, under the description of the 'abiraterone trial', Treatment Arm 1 is identified as 'standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg'. Abiraterone acetate is an androgen biosynthesis inhibitor, and prednisolone is a corticosteroid.
Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1.group_name: Class of Agent in Treatment Arm 1

Quality of Life Scale.evidence: The document details various outcomes such as overall survival, prostate cancer-specific survival, failure-free survival, progression-free survival, metastatic progression-free survival, symptomatic skeletal-related events, toxic effects, adverse events, and cost-effectiveness. However, there is no mention of a 'Quality of Life Scale' or any assessment of participants' overall well-being or satisfaction with their health status using a standardized questionnaire or survey.

Quality of Life reported.evidence: The document lists primary and secondary outcomes in the 'Outcomes' section (page 446) including overall survival, prostate cancer-specific survival, failure-free survival, progression-free survival, metastatic progression-free survival, symptomatic skeletal-related events, toxic effects and adverse events, and cost-effectiveness. Quality of life is not mentioned as an outcome or reported anywhere in the document.

NCT.evidence: Found in the 'Methods' section: 'STAMPEDE is registered with ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT00268476)'.

PubMed ID.evidence: The PubMed ID is not explicitly reported in the document.

Trial Name.value: STAMPEDE platform protocol (specifically, two randomised phase 3 trials: abiraterone trial and abiraterone and enzalutamide trial)
Trial Name.evidence: The main title of the article states 'final results from two randomised phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'. The 'Methods' section also refers to 'two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'.

Author.value: Gerhardt Attard, Laura Murphy, Noel W Clarke, Ashwin Sachdeva, Craig Jones, Alex Hoyle, William Cross, Robert J Jones, Christopher C Parker, Silke Gillessen, Adrian Cook, Chris Brawley, Clare Gilson, Hannah Rush, Hoda Abdel-Aty, Claire L Amos, Claire Murphy, Simon Chowdhury, Zafar Malik, J Martin Russell, Nazia Parkar, Cheryl Pugh, Carlos Diaz-Montana, Carmel Pezaro, Warren Grant, Helen Saxby, Ian Pedley, Joe M O'Sullivan, Alison Birtle, Joanna Gale, Narayanan Srihari, Carys Thomas, Jacob Tanguay, John Wagstaff, Prantik Das, Emma Gray, Mymoona Alzouebi, Omi Parikh, Angus Robinson, Amir H Montazeri, James Wylie, Anjali Zarkar, Richard Cathomas, Michael D Brown, Yatin Jain, David P Dearnaley*, Malcolm D Mason*, Duncan Gilbert, Ruth E Langley, Robin Millman, David Matheson, Matthew R Sydest, Louise C Brownt, Mahesh K B Parmart, Nicholas D Jamest, for the STAMPEDE investigators‡
Author.evidence: The full list of authors is provided directly under the article title.

Year.evidence: The publication year is indicated in the journal citation 'Lancet Oncol 2023; 24: 443–56'.

Full Pub or Abstract.evidence: The document contains a comprehensive structure including a summary, detailed methods, findings, interpretation, discussion, and extensive references, indicating it is a full publication rather than just an abstract.

Phase.evidence: Stated explicitly in the 'Methods' section: 'We analysed two open-label, randomised, controlled, phase 3 trials of the STAMPEDE platform protocol'.

Original/Follow Up.evidence: The title states 'final results' and the introduction mentions 'evaluate long-term outcomes' and 'assess new data on long-term efficacy' from trials that have now closed, with median follow-up times of 96 and 72 months, indicating a follow-up analysis.

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Trea.evidence: The document provides median overall survival for the treatment arms (e.g., 76.6 months for abiraterone group, 73.1 months for abiraterone and enzalutamide group) and mentions that study treatment was administered until radiographical progression or discontinuation. It also states that 23% of patients in the abiraterone trial and 22% in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial continued treatment after more than 82 months of follow-up. However, a specific 'Median On-Treatment Duration (mo)' for the entire treatment group is not explicitly reported.

Median On-Treatment Duration (mo) | Cont.evidence: The document states that the standard of care was androgen deprivation therapy, which patients were required to start no longer than 12 weeks before random assignment and was intended to be 'life-long'. It provides median overall survival for the control arms (e.g., 45.7 months in the abiraterone trial, 51.8 months in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial). However, a specific 'Median On-Treatment Duration (mo)' for the control group is not explicitly reported.

Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.value: standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg orally
Treatment Arm 1 Regimen.evidence: The 'Methods' section under 'Randomisation and masking' states: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care ... or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally'. This describes the first experimental treatment arm.

Secondary Endpoint(s).value: prostate cancer-specific survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from prostate cancer); failure-free survival (defined as biochemical failure, local or lymph node progression, distant metastases, or death from prostate cancer); progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival but excluding biochemical failure); metastatic progression-free survival (defined as progression of or new distant metastases or death from prostate cancer); symptomatic skeletal-related events (defined as pathological fracture or spinal cord compression or bone pain requiring radiotherapy or surgery); toxic effects and adverse events; and cost-effectiveness of research treatment.
Secondary Endpoint(s).evidence: The 'Outcomes' subsection under 'Methods' on page 446 explicitly states: 'Secondary outcomes were prostate cancer-specific survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from prostate cancer); failure-free survival (defined as biochemical failure, local or lymph node progression, distant metastases, or death from prostate cancer); progression-free survival (defined as failure-free survival but excluding biochemical failure); metastatic progression-free survival (defined as progression of or new distant metastases or death from prostate cancer); symptomatic skeletal-related events (defined as pathological fracture or spinal cord compression or bone pain requiring radiotherapy or surgery); toxic effects and adverse events; and cost-effectiveness of research treatment.'

Primary Endpoint(s).value: overall survival (defined as the time from randomisation to death from any cause)
Primary Endpoint(s).evidence: The 'Methods' section states: 'The primary outcome was overall survival assessed in the intention-to-treat population.' This is further clarified under the 'Outcomes' subheading: 'The primary outcome was overall survival for both trials (defined as the time from randomisation to death from any cause).'

Type of Therapy.value: Standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).
Type of Therapy.evidence: The 'Methods' section, specifically the paragraph describing patient assignment, details the different treatment arms: 'Patients were randomly assigned (1:1) ... to either standard of care (androgen deprivation therapy; docetaxel 75 mg/m² intravenously for six cycles with prednisolone 10 mg orally once per day allowed from Dec 17, 2015) or standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg (in the abiraterone trial) orally or abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg orally once a day (in the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial).'

OS Rate (%) | High volume | Treatment.evidence: The document discusses outcomes split by high-volume metastatic disease and provides median overall survival for these subgroups (Figure 3E), but does not report the OS rate as a percentage at a specific time point for the treatment arm.

OS Rate (%) | High volume | Control.evidence: The document discusses outcomes split by high-volume metastatic disease and provides median overall survival for these subgroups (Figure 3E), but does not report the OS rate as a percentage at a specific time point for the control arm.

OS Rate (%) | Low volume | Treatment.evidence: The document discusses outcomes split by low-volume metastatic disease and provides median overall survival for these subgroups (Figure 3D), but does not report the OS rate as a percentage at a specific time point for the treatment arm.

OS Rate (%) | Low volume | Control.evidence: The document discusses outcomes split by low-volume metastatic disease and provides median overall survival for these subgroups (Figure 3D), but does not report the OS rate as a percentage at a specific time point for the control arm.

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Treatment.evidence: The document reports Hazard Ratios for progression-free survival (PFS) comparing treatment to standard of care, but does not provide a specific PFS rate (percentage) for the treatment arm alone. For the abiraterone trial, the HR for PFS was 0.47 (95% CI 0.40–0.55), and for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial, it was 0.50 (95% CI 0.42–0.59), as stated in the paragraph starting "Improvements in all other secondary outcomes..." on page 10, but these are comparative measures, not individual arm rates.

PFS Rate (%) | Overall | Control.evidence: The document reports Hazard Ratios for progression-free survival (PFS) comparing treatment to standard of care, but does not provide a specific PFS rate (percentage) for the control arm alone. For the abiraterone trial, the HR for PFS was 0.47 (95% CI 0.40–0.55), and for the abiraterone and enzalutamide trial, it was 0.50 (95% CI 0.42–0.59), as stated in the paragraph starting "Improvements in all other secondary outcomes..." on page 10, but these are comparative measures, not individual arm rates.

Add-on Treatment.evidence: The 'Background' section states 'Abiraterone acetate plus prednisolone (herein referred to as abiraterone) or enzalutamide added at the start of androgen deprivation therapy improves outcomes'. The 'Methods' section describes the experimental arms as 'standard of care plus abiraterone acetate 1000 mg and prednisolone 5 mg' and 'standard of care plus abiraterone acetate and prednisolone plus enzalutamide 160 mg'. These are the additional therapies given in combination with the primary treatment (androgen deprivation therapy).
